sections.heading: Overall sourcing stayed nearly balanced between public and private channels
sections.body: Among users of modern contraceptive methods aged 15 to 49 years, 51.0 percent obtained their method from the public sector in 2025, while 41.8 percent used the private medical sector and 7.2 percent used another source. The public-sector share was almost unchanged from 50.1 percent in 2022, but the private medical sector declined from 46.3 to 41.8 percent. The offset was mainly outside medical channels, where other sources doubled from 3.6 to 7.2 percent.

sections.heading: IUDs, injectables, and implants remained strongly public-sector methods
sections.body: The most public-sector-dependent methods in 2025 were implants at 93.9 percent, IUDs at 90.4 percent, and injectables at 89.0 percent. These shares were already high in 2022, but each rose further: implants from 90.0 percent, IUDs from 88.3 percent, and injectables from 85.3 percent. This suggests a stable service-channel pattern in which methods that typically require clinical insertion or administration continued to rely heavily on public providers.

sections.heading: Public-sector detail shows different delivery points by method
sections.body: The public sector was not a single channel. Female sterilization was concentrated in government hospitals at 67.9 percent in 2025, while injectables were concentrated in barangay health stations at 57.0 percent. Implants were split mainly between rural or urban health centers and lying-in clinics at 35.6 percent and barangay health stations at 34.7 percent. IUDs had a more mixed public profile, with 47.3 percent from government hospitals, 23.8 percent from health centers or lying-in clinics, and 18.6 percent from barangay health stations.

sections.heading: Pills were still primarily obtained through pharmacies
sections.body: Pill sourcing was the clearest contrast to the public-sector pattern. In 2025, 67.0 percent of pill users obtained the method from the private medical sector, almost entirely through pharmacies at 66.0 percent. Public-sector sourcing accounted for 22.8 percent, mostly barangay health stations at 15.1 percent. Compared with 2022, pharmacy sourcing declined from 69.6 to 66.0 percent, while shop or store sourcing rose from 5.2 to 10.1 percent.

sections.heading: Male condom sourcing shifted away from pharmacies toward other retail and informal channels
sections.body: Male condoms remained more private and retail-oriented than most other methods, but the channel mix changed sharply. Pharmacy sourcing fell from 71.9 percent in 2022 to 54.2 percent in 2025, while shop or store sourcing rose from 12.6 to 29.2 percent. Other source channels also became more visible, increasing from 13.8 to 33.1 percent, including friend or relative sourcing rising from 0.4 to 3.9 percent. Because the male sterilization and other-method estimates are suppressed for source rows, condoms are the main male-oriented method that can be interpreted from this table.

sections.heading: Female sterilization remained hospital-centered with little change
sections.body: Female sterilization had one of the most stable source profiles. Public-sector sourcing was 73.5 percent in 2025, nearly the same as 73.0 percent in 2022, and government hospitals accounted for 67.9 percent of sourcing in 2025. Private hospital, clinic, or lying-in clinic sourcing accounted for another 25.4 percent. This makes female sterilization distinct from pills and condoms, which were mostly retail-based, and from injectables and implants, where barangay and health-center channels were much more prominent.

takeaways: Modern contraceptive sourcing was almost evenly split between the public sector and private medical sector in 2025.
IUDs, injectables, and implants relied heavily on public providers, while pills and male condoms depended much more on pharmacies, shops, and other nonpublic channels.
The clearest shift from 2022 to 2025 was outside medical channels, especially for male condoms and, to a lesser extent, pills.
